title: Chapter 8: The Parents' Journey
content:
"Every student crosses a border. Many parents do too, even though they never leave home."

## Opening Question

Who begins the study abroad journey?

The obvious answer is: the student.

Yet our observations suggest another reality.

Long before an application is submitted, another journey quietly begins. A father starts calculating finances. A mother begins worrying about distance. Parents discuss possibilities after their children have gone to sleep. Families imagine futures they cannot fully predict.

The student prepares to leave. The parents prepare to let go.

The two journeys happen simultaneously. One is visible. The other is often invisible.

## 8.1 Parents Rarely See Only Education

Students often describe studying abroad as an educational opportunity. Parents often see something much larger.

They see their child's safety, their family's savings, years of sacrifice, an uncertain future, a possible source of family pride, and a possible source of heartbreak.

The same decision carries different meanings for different people.

## 8.2 Hope and Fear Can Exist Together

One of the most powerful observations about parents is that hope and fear frequently exist at the same time.

A parent may hope their child receives a visa. The same parent may fear losing daily contact. They may celebrate an offer letter, then quietly worry about loneliness. They may encourage independence, while privately wondering who will care for their child in another country.

Love often creates contradictory emotions. Supporting a child's future sometimes means accepting personal uncertainty.

> **Atlas Concept 8.1 — The Parental Paradox**
>
> The Parental Paradox describes the experience of simultaneously wanting a child to grow while fearing the distance that growth may create.
>
> Parents therefore experience both pride and grief throughout the study abroad journey. Neither emotion cancels the other. They coexist.

## 8.3 The Invisible Financial Story

Money appears frequently in conversations about studying abroad — usually as tuition, living expenses, bank statements, loans, and financial documents.

Our observations suggest another perspective.

Behind every financial document is a family story. Years of savings. Business decisions. Property sold. Loans accepted. Dreams postponed.

Financial commitment is rarely only financial. It often represents trust in the child's future.

## 8.4 Parents Carry Their Own Uncertainty

Students are not the only people asking questions. Parents quietly ask their own.

*Will my child be safe? Will they be happy? Will they succeed? Will they return? Did we make the right decision?*

Unlike students, parents often ask these questions privately. Their uncertainty receives far less attention. Yet it may be equally significant.

> **Atlas Concept 8.2 — Shared Uncertainty**
>
> Shared Uncertainty occurs when multiple members of a family experience different uncertainties about the same decision.
>
> The student worries about admission. The parents worry about the student's future. Both experience uncertainty. But not the same uncertainty.

## 8.5 Parents Also Borrow Confidence

Students are not the only people influenced by stories. Parents seek reassurance too.

They speak with relatives, neighbours, friends, other parents, and families whose children have already gone abroad. These conversations rarely focus only on procedures. They focus on outcomes.

Parents are often asking one silent question: *"Will my child be okay?"*

## 8.6 Letting Go Is Also a Journey

Departure day is often described from the student's perspective: packing, airports, flights, excitement.

Yet another journey begins at the airport. Parents return home. A bedroom becomes quiet. Daily routines change. Video calls replace conversations at dinner.

The journey continues for those who stay behind. Their transformation deserves recognition.

## Atlas Model 8.1 — The Dual Journey Model

```
       Student                    Parents
      ________                  __________
       Dream                      Hope
         ↓                          ↓
    Preparation                 Sacrifice
         ↓                          ↓
     Departure                   Waiting
         ↓                          ↓
    Adaptation                 Adjustment
         ↓                          ↓
      Growth                      Pride
```

The two journeys remain connected even while taking place in different countries.

## Practical Implications

**For Students:** Remember that your parents are also adapting. Communicating regularly is not simply a habit. It is part of helping your family complete their own journey.

**For Parents:** Mixed emotions are normal. Pride does not eliminate worry. Supporting a child's independence does not require suppressing your own feelings.

**For Educators:** Parents are educational stakeholders. Helping families understand the emotional journey creates stronger support systems for students.

**For Counselors:** Counseling should include parents whenever appropriate. Many concerns that appear academic are actually family concerns.

## Counterargument

Family structures differ greatly. Some students receive strong parental support. Others make decisions independently. Still others navigate disagreement or limited family involvement.

The concepts presented in this chapter therefore describe recurring patterns rather than universal experiences. Future research should explore these differences more deeply.

## Chapter Summary

The study abroad journey belongs to more than the student. Parents experience their own psychological journey marked by hope, uncertainty, sacrifice, adjustment, and pride.

Recognizing this invisible journey creates a more complete understanding of international education. Students do not travel alone. Neither do parents. Each carries part of the same dream.

## Atlas Statements

- Every departure creates two journeys: one abroad and one at home.
- Parents invest more than money. They invest trust.
- The airport is not only where students leave. It is also where parents learn to let go.
- Behind every successful student is a family adapting to change.
- Studying abroad transforms families, not only individuals.
